title: Failure-safe cleanup

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Failure-safe cleanup; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: SEI CERT C Coding Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Failure-safe cleanup
sources.claim: C resource management assigns clear ownership and routes every success or failure path through cleanup that releases each acquired resource once in reverse dependency order.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C resource management assigns clear ownership and routes every success or failure path through cleanup that releases each acquired resource once in reverse dependency order.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A function opens a file, allocates a buffer, and on any later failure jumps to one cleanup section that frees the buffer then closes the file. Your goal is to repair a multi-resource function to preserve the first failure and release all acquired resources. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Pointers, arrays and arithmetic; Structures; Allocation and deallocation; File streams and binary/text modes. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C resource management assigns clear ownership and routes every success or failure path through cleanup that releases each acquired resource once in reverse dependency order. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Early returns, partially initialized aggregates, callbacks, signals, and ownership transfer complicate cleanup and require explicit state flags or helper abstractions. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: The operating system eventually reclaims resources, so cleanup on error paths is unnecessary in a C program.

includes: Structure cleanup so partially acquired resources are released exactly once.
Repair a multi-resource function to preserve the first failure and release all acquired resources.
excludes: Early returns, partially initialized aggregates, callbacks, signals, and ownership transfer complicate cleanup and require explicit state flags or helper abstractions.

outcomes: Repair a multi-resource function to preserve the first failure and release all acquired resources.

examples.title: Failure-safe cleanup: defined case
examples.situation: A function opens a file, allocates a buffer, and on any later failure jumps to one cleanup section that frees the buffer then closes the file.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A function opens a file, allocates a buffer, and on any later failure jumps to one cleanup section that frees the buffer then closes the file.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Release every owned resource on every path; process cleanup is not a substitute for bounded files, locks, memory, or long-running correctness.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Release every owned resource on every path; process cleanup is not a substitute for bounded files, locks, memory, or long-running correctness.

examples.title: Failure-safe cleanup: boundary case
examples.situation: A pointer is set to null after ownership transfers so the former owner does not free the same allocation twice.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A pointer is set to null after ownership transfers so the former owner does not free the same allocation twice.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Early returns, partially initialized aggregates, callbacks, signals, and ownership transfer complicate cleanup and require explicit state flags or helper abstractions.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: The operating system eventually reclaims resources, so cleanup on error paths is unnecessary in a C program.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Repair a multi-resource function to preserve the first failure and release all acquired resources.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The operating system eventually reclaims resources, so cleanup on error paths is unnecessary in a C program.
misconceptions.correction: Release every owned resource on every path; process cleanup is not a substitute for bounded files, locks, memory, or long-running correctness.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: The operating system eventually reclaims resources, so cleanup on error paths is unnecessary in a C program.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Failure-safe cleanup: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Early returns, partially initialized aggregates, callbacks, signals, and ownership transfer complicate cleanup and require explicit state flags or helper abstractions.
